strands.title: System Prompt Waste Analysis Project Scope & Phase Transition
strands.content: A system prompt analyzer module (src/pichay/analyzer.py) is being planned to measure waste and inefficiency in API calls across logged sessions. The core premise: system prompts are often reused verbatim, contain unused components, and have duplicated content—all representing wasted context tokens. The project focuses on instrumenting existing proxy JSONL logs to decompose prompts and measure waste quantitatively. This integrates into a larger pichay system already containing proxy.py (log capture) and eval.py (evaluation framework). The analyzer should produce structured results with dataclasses and expose functionality via CLI. **CYCLE 5 INTEGRATION: PRIORITY 1 task execution has begun. The [tool_result: Read] block has been received with the opening of proxy.py source code. This represents the transition from 'awaiting code content' to 'in receipt of code content and actively extracting findings.' The file header indicates proxy.py is a logging proxy for Claude API calls with two operational modes: Observe (default, logs request/response metrics, pure output) and presumably a second mode.** **CYCLE 6 INTEGRATION: A second [tool_use: Read] is present in new content, indicating user intends further code reading. However, NO NEW CODE CONTENT has been provided—only the tool_use marker. This represents a state of 'awaiting results from the read operation.' The tensor must track that: (1) a new read operation has been initiated, (2) results are not yet visible, (3) future content will likely contain code snippets from proxy.py or eval.py, (4) the system is in a 'pending read results' state. This cycle transitions from 'receiving code' to 'awaiting next code delivery.' The strand integrates this by noting the operational pause while maintaining PRIORITY 1 momentum.** **CYCLE 7 INTEGRATION: Breakthrough—eval.py has now been opened. [tool_result: Read] delivers lines 1-5 of eval.py header. The docstring describes eval.py as 'Evaluation framework for context paging experiments. Parses proxy JSONL logs from experimental runs, computes per-turn and cumulative metrics...' This is CRITICAL: eval.py is the CONSUMER of proxy.py logs. It depends on the JSONL schema from proxy.py. The project structure is now visible: proxy.py generates JSONL, eval.py consumes and analyzes it, and analyzer.py will be a NEW component within this pipeline. The code examination has progressed from proxy.py header (partial) to eval.py header (new), confirming the pichay system architecture.

key_claims.text: System prompts in API logs contain multiple distinct semantic components (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) that can be pattern-matched and decomposed
Most tool definitions are static across turns, while components like git status are dynamic; re-sending identical static components wastes tokens
A significant portion of tool definitions in system prompts are never invoked in actual tool_use blocks in messages
Skills and other semantic components appear multiple times (often tripled) within a single system prompt, representing duplicate waste
The project is now in operational execution phase with externally-persistent todo list; todos have been successfully created and are ready to be advanced
PRIORITY 1 task (read/analyze proxy.py and eval.py) is now actively receiving code content; proxy.py partial, eval.py now opened at cycle 7
eval.py is a downstream consumer of proxy.py JSONL logs, designed to parse experimental runs and compute per-turn cumulative metrics

strands.title: Functional Requirements & Decomposition Strategy—Now Task-Backed
strands.content: The analyzer must implement five core analyses: (1) COMPONENT DECOMPOSITION—parse system prompt text and segment it by semantic block using pattern matching on text boundaries. (2) STATIC vs DYNAMIC ANALYSIS—track whether each component changes across turns within a session; measure bytes of static content re-sent unnecessarily. (3) TOOL USAGE TRACKING—extract tool definitions from system prompt, cross-reference against actual tool_use blocks in message records, identify defined-but-unused tools, and sum their byte cost. (4) DUPLICATE DETECTION—find identical or near-identical semantic blocks appearing multiple times in a single prompt; report byte counts. (5) PER-SESSION SUMMARY—aggregate metrics as absolute bytes and percentages. The module must use dataclasses to structure results, expose a CLI entry point, and require careful study of proxy.py and eval.py before implementation. **CYCLE 5-6 INTEGRATION: Code examination strategy remains active. After cycle 5's partial receipt of proxy.py header (lines 1-5), cycle 6 shows [tool_use: Read] marker without new code content, indicating the user is queuing another read operation. The requirements remain valid and unchanged; implementation remains blocked pending full schema understanding.** **CYCLE 7 INTEGRATION: eval.py header now available (lines 1-5). eval.py is identified as an 'evaluation framework for context paging experiments'—it PARSES proxy JSONL logs and COMPUTES metrics. This confirms the dependency chain: proxy.py → JSONL logs → eval.py consumes them → analyzer.py will extend/augment this pipeline with waste-specific metrics. The functional decomposition strategy continues to be the framework guiding PRIORITY 1 and future PRIORITY 2-3 tasks. Cycle 7 brings architectural clarity: the analyzer.py is part of a METRICS PIPELINE, not a standalone tool.

key_claims.text: Component decomposition must rely on pattern matching over heuristic text segmentation to identify tool definitions, skill blocks, and metadata sections
Static vs dynamic analysis requires tracking component state across multiple turns within a session to measure re-transmission waste
Tool usage tracking depends on correctly parsing tool_use blocks in message JSON and matching their names against tool definitions in the system prompt
Duplicate detection should quantify byte cost of repeated semantic blocks, likely to reveal skills being tripled or other structural redundancy
Per-session summary metrics should report both absolute byte counts and percentages to allow comparative analysis across sessions and cost models
analyzer.py will be designed as an extension to the existing metrics pipeline (proxy.py + eval.py), following established patterns for dataclass-based structured results and CLI exposure

strands.title: Code Examination Phase 1-2 Transition: Proxy.py & eval.py Discovery
strands.content: In cycle 5, proxy.py code content was received (lines 1-5, partial): file header #!/usr/bin/env python3, docstring describing 'logging proxy for Claude API calls, with optional context paging,' and two operational modes identified: (1) Observe (default)—logs request/response metrics, produces pure output. A second mode was mentioned but not fully documented in the received excerpt. **CYCLE 6**: [tool_use: Read] marker without delivered content indicated a queued read operation; results were pending. **CYCLE 7 BREAKTHROUGH**: eval.py is now opened and its first lines delivered: #!/usr/bin/env python3 (line 1), docstring header (lines 2-5) stating 'Evaluation framework for context paging experiments. Parses proxy JSONL logs from experimental runs, computes per-turn and cumulative metrics...' (text truncated mid-word after 'metrics'). This ESTABLISHES THE CHAIN: proxy.py is the logging layer, eval.py is the analysis/metrics layer, and the analyzer.py (to be created) will add waste-specific decomposition on top. The Phase 2 code examination has now expanded to TWO FILES. The eval.py header reveals the PURPOSE of the pichay system: it is designed to measure and analyze 'context paging experiments'—meaning the system is being instrumented to study how context is managed, what wastes exist, and how paging strategies affect API efficiency. The analyzer.py project sits at the intersection of this instrumentation need. Key architectural insight: both proxy.py and eval.py follow the #!/usr/bin/env python3 + docstring pattern, suggesting CLI-driven design with documentation baked into source.

key_claims.text: proxy.py is a CLI-based logging proxy that captures Claude API calls for observation and analysis
eval.py is an evaluation framework specifically designed to parse proxy JSONL logs and compute per-turn/cumulative metrics for context paging experiments
proxy.py has at least two operational modes: Observe (default, logging-only) and one other (unnamed/not fully described)
In Observe mode, proxy.py logs request/response metrics without modifying context data
proxy.py is designed as an executable Python 3 script, suitable for CLI invocation from the command line
The pichay system is designed to instrument and study context paging experiments, measuring efficiency and waste in API calls
Cycle 7 delivers eval.py lines 1-5; complete source remains pending for both proxy.py and eval.py

strands.title: Execution State & Task Momentum: Cycle 7 Code Delivery Acceleration
strands.content: PRIORITY 1 task remains active and has ACCELERATED DELIVERY. Cycle 5 received proxy.py header (lines 1-5); Cycle 6 showed pending read marker; Cycle 7 DELIVERS eval.py header (lines 1-5, partial). The system is now transitioning from 'awaiting individual deliveries' to 'receiving continuous code streams.' The tensor tracks: (1) PRIORITY 1 status = 'in progress, actively receiving code from TWO SOURCE FILES (proxy.py partial + eval.py new),' (2) code examination progress = advanced (proxy.py header extracted; eval.py now opened; full sources still pending but architecture is becoming visible), (3) findings checkpoint = 'architectural validation confirmed: proxy.py→JSONL→eval.py→analyzer.py is the dependency chain,' (4) PRIORITY 2-3 remain blocked but architectural foundation is solidifying, (5) next expected deliveries = continuation of eval.py code (to understand dataclass patterns and CLI conventions) and/or return to proxy.py for JSONL schema details. The execution phase has established momentum across multiple files. The todo list from cycle 4-5 remains active. Cycle 8 should expect either: (a) more eval.py code (likely the main function and dataclass definitions), (b) return to proxy.py for full schema exploration, or (c) code samples of actual JSONL records to ground understanding. The tensor must maintain readiness to synthesize findings once code saturation reaches critical threshold.

key_claims.text: PRIORITY 1 task is actively in-progress; code examination is now accelerating with deliveries from TWO files (proxy.py, eval.py)
Cycle 7 delivers eval.py lines 1-5, confirming eval.py is an evaluation framework that parses proxy JSONL logs and computes metrics
The architectural dependency chain is now visible: proxy.py (logging) → JSONL logs → eval.py (metrics) → analyzer.py (new, waste-specific analysis)
Both proxy.py and eval.py follow the #!/usr/bin/env python3 + docstring pattern, suggesting CLI-driven design with baked-in documentation
Complete source code for proxy.py and eval.py is still pending; only headers (lines 1-5) have been received so far
Cycle 8 should expect continuation of code delivery from eval.py (dataclass patterns, CLI conventions) or return to proxy.py (JSONL schema details)
